name: typescript
context: TypeScript language ecosystem — compiler, type system, generics, project configuration, and module resolution for typed JavaScript
info: TypeScript is a typed superset of JavaScript that compiles to plain JS. It adds static types, interfaces, generics, enums, and advanced type-level programming to JavaScript. TypeScript is the primary language for building production web applications, Node.js services, and AI agent tooling in the JavaScript ecosystem. The compiler (tsc) performs type checking and emits JavaScript, while editors use the TypeScript Language Service for real-time feedback. TypeScript's type system is structural (not nominal) — two types are compatible if their shapes match, not their names. Every valid JavaScript file is valid TypeScript, making incremental adoption straightforward. The ecosystem centers on tsconfig.json for project configuration, the tsc CLI for compilation, and the npm/pnpm/yarn package managers for distribution. TypeScript 5.x is the current major version, featuring decorators (5.0), const type parameters (5.0), satisfies operator (4.9), verbatimModuleSyntax (5.0), and per-file emit with isolatedDeclarations (5.5). Declaration files (.d.ts) provide type information for JavaScript libraries via DefinitelyTyped (@types/* packages).
body:
# TypeScript Ecosystem

TypeScript adds static types to JavaScript. It catches bugs at compile time, enables powerful editor tooling (autocomplete, refactoring, go-to-definition), and documents code intent through type annotations. Every JavaScript runtime (Node.js, Deno, Bun, browsers) can execute TypeScript's compiled output.

## Quick Start

```bash
# Install TypeScript
npm install -D typescript

# Initialize a tsconfig.json
npx tsc --init

# Type-check without emitting
npx tsc --noEmit

# Compile to JavaScript
npx tsc

# Watch mode
npx tsc --watch
```

## TypeScript Versions

| Version | Key Features for Agents |
|---------|------------------------|
| 4.7 | ESM support in Node.js (`moduleResolution: "node16"`), `moduleSuffixes` |
| 4.9 | `satisfies` operator, auto-accessors, `in` narrowing improvements |
| 5.0 | ECMAScript decorators, `const` type parameters, `--moduleResolution bundler` |
| 5.1 | Easier implicit returns for `undefined`, unrelated types in getters/setters |
| 5.2 | `using` declarations (explicit resource management), decorator metadata |
| 5.3 | Import attributes, `resolution-mode` in import types, `--isolatedDeclarations` preview |
| 5.4 | `NoInfer<T>` utility type, `Object.groupBy`/`Map.groupBy` types, preserved narrowing in closures |
| 5.5 | `isolatedDeclarations`, inferred type predicates, `${ConfigName}` in config paths |
| 5.6 | `--noCheck` flag, region-prioritized diagnostics, `--build --clean` |
| 5.7 | `--rewriteRelativeImportExtensions`, `--target es2024`, path rewriting improvements |
| 5.8 | `--module nodenext` default improvements, `--erasableSyntaxOnly`, `require()` of ESM in Node 22+ |

## File Extensions

| Extension | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| `.ts` | TypeScript source file |
| `.tsx` | TypeScript with JSX (React components) |
| `.d.ts` | Type declaration file (no runtime code) |
| `.mts` | TypeScript ES module source |
| `.cts` | TypeScript CommonJS source |
| `.d.mts` | ES module declaration file |
| `.d.cts` | CommonJS declaration file |

## Structural Typing

TypeScript uses structural typing — compatibility is determined by shape, not name:

```typescript
interface Point {
  x: number;
  y: number;
}

function distance(p: Point): number {
  return Math.sqrt(p.x ** 2 + p.y ** 2);
}

// Works — { x, y } matches Point's shape
const p = { x: 3, y: 4, label: "origin" };
distance(p); // OK, has x and y
```

## Declaration Files and DefinitelyTyped

JavaScript libraries ship type definitions as `.d.ts` files. If a library doesn't include its own types, install them from DefinitelyTyped:

```bash
# Types bundled with the library (nothing extra needed)
npm install axios  # ships with types

# Types from DefinitelyTyped
npm install express
npm install -D @types/express

# Check if types exist
npm info @types/lodash
```

## Project Structure

A typical TypeScript project:

```
my-project/
  tsconfig.json           # Compiler configuration
  package.json            # Dependencies and scripts
  src/
    index.ts              # Entry point
    types.ts              # Shared type definitions
    utils/
      helpers.ts
  tests/
    index.test.ts
  dist/                   # Compiled output (git-ignored)
    index.js
    index.d.ts
```

## Running TypeScript Directly

Several runtimes execute TypeScript without a separate compile step:

```bash
# Node.js 22.6+ (experimental strip-only transform)
node --experimental-strip-types index.ts

# tsx (Node.js wrapper, most popular)
npx tsx index.ts
npx tsx watch index.ts

# ts-node (older, needs config)
npx ts-node index.ts

# Deno (native TypeScript support)
deno run index.ts

# Bun (native TypeScript support)
bun run index.ts
```

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|----------|
| `TS_NODE_PROJECT` | Path to tsconfig.json for ts-node |
| `TS_NODE_COMPILER_OPTIONS` | Override compiler options in ts-node (JSON string) |
| `TSC_WATCHFILE` | File watching strategy: `UseFsEvents`, `UseFsEventsOnParentDirectory`, `DynamicPriorityPolling` |
| `TSC_NONPOLLING_WATCHER` | Set to `1` to use native file watchers instead of polling |

## Child Nodes

- **[[typescript/compiler]]** — The `tsc` CLI: compiling, type-checking, watch mode, build mode, and diagnostics
- **[[typescript/config]]** — `tsconfig.json` configuration: compiler options, paths, module resolution, and project setup
- **[[typescript/types]]** — The type system: primitives, unions, intersections, narrowing, utility types, and type-level programming
- **[[typescript/generics]]** — Generic functions, classes, constraints, conditional types, and inference patterns
- **[[typescript/project-refs]]** — Project references, composite projects, build mode, and monorepo TypeScript architecture

sections.title: Type system overview
sections.body: TypeScript's type system is structural and gradual. Structural means compatibility is based on shape (matching properties), not declared type names. Gradual means you can opt out with `any` or opt in to stricter checking with `strict: true` in tsconfig. The system supports union types (`string | number`), intersection types (`A & B`), literal types (`"hello"` or `42`), tuple types (`[string, number]`), conditional types (`T extends U ? X : Y`), mapped types (`{ [K in keyof T]: V }`), and template literal types (`` `${string}Id` ``). See [[typescript/types]] for full coverage.

sections.title: Compilation pipeline
sections.body: TypeScript compilation has three phases: parsing (source to AST), type checking (type inference and error reporting), and emit (AST to JavaScript + declaration files). The `--noEmit` flag skips emit for type-check-only workflows. The `--isolatedModules` flag ensures each file can be transpiled independently (required for Babel, swc, esbuild). The `--declaration` flag generates `.d.ts` files for library consumers. See [[typescript/compiler]] for CLI usage and build mode.

sections.title: Module systems
sections.body: TypeScript supports both CommonJS (`require`/`module.exports`) and ES modules (`import`/`export`). The `module` compiler option controls output format: `commonjs`, `es2015`/`es2020`/`es2022`/`esnext`, `node16`, `nodenext`. The `moduleResolution` option controls how imports are resolved: `node` (legacy Node.js), `node16`/`nodenext` (modern Node.js with ESM support), `bundler` (for webpack/vite/esbuild). Use `moduleResolution: "bundler"` for frontend projects and `moduleResolution: "nodenext"` for Node.js libraries. See [[typescript/config]] for full module configuration.

sections.title: Tooling ecosystem
sections.body: TypeScript integrates with the broader JavaScript tooling ecosystem. Build tools: tsc (official), esbuild (fast bundler with TS support), swc (Rust-based transpiler), Vite (dev server using esbuild). Linters: ESLint with @typescript-eslint/parser and @typescript-eslint/eslint-plugin, or Biome (all-in-one). Formatters: Prettier, Biome. Test runners: Vitest (Vite-native), Jest with ts-jest or @swc/jest, Node.js built-in test runner. Package managers: npm, pnpm, yarn. Monorepo tools: Turborepo, Nx, Lerna. All major editors provide TypeScript language service integration via tsserver.

links.context: The tsc CLI — compile, type-check, watch, and build TypeScript projects
tsconfig.json — all compiler options, paths, module resolution, and project setup
The type system — primitives, unions, intersections, narrowing, utility types
Generic functions, classes, constraints, conditional types, and inference
Project references — composite builds, monorepos, incremental compilation

related.note: Node.js is the primary runtime for TypeScript server-side code
The Anthropic TypeScript SDK (@anthropic-ai/sdk) is a primary client for building Claude agents
MCP's TypeScript SDK uses TypeScript for server and client implementations
Python is the other major language for AI agent development alongside TypeScript

author: dojo-community
